description: This figure presents a comprehensive microstructural characterization of Fe-26Mn-xAl-1C low-density steels (x = 3, 6, 10, 12 wt% Al) after solution treatment at 1100°C for 1 hour and water quenching. Panels (a)-(d) display optical micrographs revealing the grain structure evolution with increasing Al content: 3Al and 6Al steels exhibit fully austenitic microstructures with similar grain sizes (~80 μm excluding twin boundaries), while 10Al steel shows austenite with extensive annealing twins, and 12Al steel contains approximately 7 vol% ferrite (appearing as small white blocky phase) with refined austenite grains (~40 μm, or ~16 μm including twin boundaries). Panels (e) and (f) show TEM bright-field images and corresponding selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns for 10Al and 12Al steels, respectively. The SAED pattern in (e) exhibits weak superlattice spots indicating the presence of ordered (Fe,Mn)3AlCx (κ) carbides in 10Al steel, whereas (f) displays strong superlattice spots and two sets of modulation contrast in 12Al steel, evidencing extensive κ-carbide precipitation even in the as-quenched condition. These observations establish that increasing Al content promotes κ-carbide formation when exceeding 10 wt%, contributing significantly to the dramatic yield strength increase from 470 MPa (10Al) to 952 MPa (12Al) through precipitation strengthening.
